opp.name: Barnes&Noble_Sourcing+CLM+ANA_April'26
opp.next_step: 5/12/26 RH RFI released 5/11/26 Due date June 08th. Intent to bid submitted 5/12/26
opp.ais_why:
• Salesforce strategy spells it out: stress 'the workforce multiplier for agentic AI especially ANA' and use CLM to cut contract cycle times 70%.
• On the April 7 intro, B&N agreed an RFI would land in May, with autonomous negotiation framed as the way a four-person team can tackle $500M of unsourced indirect spend.
• RFI was issued 5/11 with intent-to-bid already submitted; active formal evaluation tied directly to ANA gives high confidence.

sources: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, speaker Allison Baker (is_rep: false)

Avoma note uuid 550d528b-68ef-4b30-801d-102d4c5c707d: 'Over $500 million in indirect spend remains unsourced by a small four-member team that must maximize savings without headcount increases through automation and standardization'
metrics.narrative: Business problem clearly articulated with partial quantification. Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) stated on the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call: 'north of half a billion dollars in indirect spend that has not been strategically sourced… we're missing out on a lot of opportunity' and 'we're able to address some of it but if we bring in an eSourcing platform that helps us standardize and execute quicker, we can bring more savings to the company for the bottom line.' Pain quantified at spend volume ($500M+ unsourced) and team constraint (4-person team, no headcount growth approved), but no specific savings target, cycle-time reduction goal, or ROI commitment stated. Buyer emphasized 'there's so much opportunity' and the manual/spreadsheet-driven process is blocking scale. Inference: savings materiality is high given spend base, but buyer has not yet built or shared a quantified business case. Next move is to co-develop a savings/ROI model during RFP response or shortlist phase, tying platform ROI to their stated $500M opportunity and current manual-process cost (labor, delayed sourcing, lost savings).

sources: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Allison Baker led the call, multiple advocacy statements
Avoma note uuid 2b110d2e-79b5-4e42-95f4-47888d9f2551: 'Agreement reached to execute an NDA and include Zycus in the May RFP process'
Next Step 08 Jun 2026: RFI released on Allison's timeline, Zycus intent submitted
LastActivityDate 09 Jun 2026 (17 days ago, within acceptable RFI-phase lag)
champion.narrative: Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) is a confirmed, active champion with strong business-buyer authority, direct access to budget approval, and clear advocacy for Zycus, though her access to the Economic Buyer and ability to drive C-suite engagement is not yet demonstrated. Evidence of championship: (1) Allison personally led the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call, articulated the pain and business case unprompted, and committed on the call to include Zycus in the May RFP and execute the NDA ('we'd love to include you all in our RFP… Zycus checks the boxes of what we're looking for'); (2) She drove the RFI release on schedule (11 May) and Zycus submitted intent to bid on time (12 May per Next Step); (3) She has budget (stated 'we did get a budget approved for our next fiscal year') and a mandate to build the procurement function from scratch ('I was brought in to start and build up an indirect procurement function that hadn't existed before'); (4) She expressed urgency and a clear implementation timeline (Q2-Q3 fall 2026); (5) She proactively asked fit questions (modularity, Oracle integration, iSaaS approach, scalability, Waterstones harmonization) and confirmed Zycus aligns ('Zycus checks the boxes'). She also demonstrated procurement-platform buying experience ('I've implemented eSourcing platforms at two different companies before… familiar with Oracle, Ariba, Workday/Scout'), so she is a credible evaluator and internal advocate. Risks/limitations: (1) Allison is at Manager level, not VP or C-suite — she can drive the evaluation and recommendation but likely does not hold final commercial sign-off authority (see EB gap); (2) Single-threading risk — only Allison and Asad Rizvi (Analyst) have engaged; no IT, Finance, Legal, or executive sponsor visible; (3) Cooling risk is LOW but present — last buyer touch was 09 Jun (17 days ago, within RFI window lag, acceptable), but no call or email since the 07 Apr discovery call (79 days gap between discovery and RFI due date is long, though explained by buyer's internal RFI-build process). Current engagement: ACTIVE — Allison is driving the process on her stated timeline. Strength: DEVELOPING — she is a strong business champion with budget and advocacy, but lacks demonstrated C-suite access or multi-threading, so her ability to shepherd the deal through final EB/CFO approval is unproven. Next move: during RFP or shortlist, ask Allison for an intro to her director (or the VP-level sponsor who approved the FY budget), position it as validating the strategic fit and ROI at the executive level, and multi-thread to IT (Scott Konzelmann, Senior Director SAP Business Systems) and Finance (Jennifer Banks, Director FP&A or Arnaud Lingelser, Finance Director) to de-risk the single-thread dependency.

sources: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Allison Baker: 'we're reaching out to different players in the market'
Competitors__c field: empty
No competition intel in any Salesforce free-text field, Next Step, Task, Event, or call transcript
competition.narrative: No competitor named by the buyer or visible in Salesforce; competitive landscape is unknown. On the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call, Allison Baker stated she has 'worked with different platforms throughout the industry, like Oracle, Ariba, Workday which used to be Scout,' indicating familiarity with incumbent/legacy platforms, but she did not name any vendor currently being evaluated alongside Zycus for this RFP. She said 'we're not married to anyone, we want to see what's out there,' implying an open competitive process, and 'we are reaching out to different players in the market to have these introduction calls,' confirming multiple vendors are in the mix. Competitors__c field in Salesforce is empty. No other Salesforce field (Existing_vendor__c, Replacing_What__c, How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c, Why_not_Zycus__c, Closed_Lost_Reason_Code__c) contains competition intel. No Next Step, Task, or Event references a competitor by name. The RFI released 11 May to multiple vendors will reveal the shortlist once scoring is complete; Zycus does not yet know the field. Inference: This is a genuine competitive RFI/RFP process with likely 3-6 vendors invited (typical for a $250k procurement-tech buy); probable competitors based on Allison's stated experience and the eSourcing+CLM+Analytics scope include SAP Ariba, Oracle Procurement Cloud, Workday Strategic Sourcing, Coupa, Jaggaer, and GEP, plus potentially newer entrants like Zip, Ivalua, or Bonfire. Zycus's wedge: modular approach (start with eSourcing, grow over time), low-IT-burden iSaaS integration (vs. Ariba/Oracle/Workday's heavier IT lift), and agentic AI for tail-spend automation (a differentiation Allison showed interest in: 'we're retail, I feel like there's a lot of benefit an auction platform could bring'). Threat level UNKNOWN until shortlist is announced. Next move: after RFI scoring (likely late Jun to mid-Jul), ask Allison who advanced to shortlist and what the evaluation committee scored as Zycus's strengths and gaps vs. peers, then tailor the RFP response and demo to widen the lead on strengths (modularity, iSaaS, AI) and close gaps.

sources: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Allison Baker (is_rep: false), multiple statements

Avoma note uuid c01bf47f-75fe-43ce-9408-05149712df54: 'Barnes & Noble's indirect procurement function has been newly established with no existing tech foundation, prompting a search for eSourcing and procurement platforms'

Avoma note uuid 550d528b-68ef-4b30-801d-102d4c5c707d: 'Over $500 million in indirect spend remains unsourced by a small four-member team that must maximize savings without headcount increases through automation and standardization'

Avoma note uuid 504.105 span: 'Current processes are highly manual—lacking purchase orders, two- or three-way matching, and relying on piecemeal systems—driving the need for eSourcing, CLM, and P2P solutions in FY starting May'
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is acute, operational, and owned by Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement). On the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call, Allison described the pain in detail: 'when I came in [~18 months ago], there's absolutely nothing, no foundation… most employees here have worked here their entire careers, 30 years plus, very old school mentality… we don't have purchase orders today on our indirect side, no three-way match, I don't even think we do a two-way match — invoice comes in, someone approves it, it gets paid.' She continued: 'north of half a billion dollars in indirect spend that has not been strategically sourced… there's so much opportunity here and we're able to address some of it, but if we bring in an eSourcing platform that helps us standardize and execute quicker, we can bring more savings to the company for the bottom line.' The pain is multi-dimensional: (1) Manual, spreadsheet-driven sourcing ('labor intensive to build RFQs'), blocking throughput and scale; (2) No P2P foundation (no POs, no matching, high maverick-spend risk); (3) Severe resource constraint (4-person team, $500M+ spend, 'they're not keen to increase headcount'); (4) Lost savings opportunity (unsourced spend = leakage); (5) Inability to scale with company growth ('you're gonna start leveraging some technology but still maintain traditions… rapid expansion puts pressure on supply chain, manual steps don't support that'). Pain owner is Allison Baker, who was hired 18 months ago explicitly to 'start and help build up an indirect procurement function that hadn't really existed before.' She has executive mandate (budget approved for FY starting May 2026) and urgency (targeting Q2-Q3 fall 2026 implementation). The pain is NOT inferred — it is buyer-stated, quantified, and tied to a business outcome (savings, scale, efficiency). Compelling event: fiscal-year start May 2026 + rapid company growth + Waterstones harmonization aspiration create a clear window to act now.

sources: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Allison Baker: 'Send our NDA and have your side review it'
Avoma note uuid a19e23e1-616f-4b71-a962-645463ec8c8e: 'Send our NDA and have your side review it'
Avoma note uuid 607d8486-4e6b-4477-a6a2-740e8bf4a049: 'Barnes & Noble will send Zycus's legal the NDA once Assad returns, targeting review and RFP kickoff in May'
Next Step 08 Jun 2026: RFI submitted (implies NDA executed by mid-May)
paper_process.narrative: NDA executed; full contracting mechanics (MSA, SOW, Order Form, legal/InfoSec review, procurement channel) not yet discussed. On the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call, Allison Baker committed to send Zycus's NDA to Barnes & Noble legal for review ('Send our NDA and have your side review it,' Avoma note uuid a19e23e1), with Asad Rizvi to follow up once back from sick leave. The Next Step dated 08 Jun reflects RFI submission, implying NDA was executed by mid-May (a reasonable inference, as RFI participation typically requires NDA in place, though not explicitly confirmed in the record). No discussion of: MSA or Order Form terms, whether Barnes & Noble uses a standard vendor agreement template, InfoSec questionnaire or security review process, whether IT will gate the purchase on integration or architecture review, or whether a partner/SI (e.g. an implementation firm or a procurement-advisory partner) will be involved. Allison did note Barnes & Noble is 'very old school' with many on-prem systems and a complex, piecemeal integration landscape (Oracle Financials, Concur invoicing, AS/400 WMS, legacy systems), and she asked about Zycus's Oracle integration experience; Hunsinger described Zycus iSaaS (integration-as-a-service) approach, to which she responded positively. Inference: integration and IT vetting will be a gate, but it has not yet been formalized into the process. No forcing function identified (no current-contract expiration, no compliance deadline). Next move: during RFP or shortlist, ask Allison to outline the contract and legal review process, the InfoSec requirements, and whether IT or a sourcing-advisory partner will participate in vendor selection, so Zycus can prep standard responses (SOW, security docs, integration plan) in advance and avoid late-cycle delays.

economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer not identified. No EB, CFO, or C-level executive has appeared on any call or in any Salesforce activity. The 07 Apr 2026 discovery call was led by Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) and attended by Asad Rizvi (Data Analyst, Indirect Procurement, off sick that day). Allison stated 'we did get a budget approved for our next fiscal year' (FY starts May 2026), indicating budget authority EXISTS and was exercised at a higher level, but she did not name who approved it or who controls the commercial envelope for this $250k procurement-tech purchase. No mention of CFO, VP Supply Chain, or C-suite sponsor. Allison also referenced 'they're not keen to increase headcount,' implying budget decisions are made above her level. The org chart visible from the account contact list includes June Johnson (VP Vendor Relations & AP) and a departed Director Strategic Sourcing (Andrew Chao, marked #Gone), but neither has surfaced. Inference: EB is likely June Johnson (VP) or a finance/operations executive; budget is approved in principle (a positive signal), but the individual with commercial sign-off authority for this deal is unmapped. This is a material gap — without EB access, pricing and commercial negotiation cannot proceed, and forecast defensibility is low. Next move: map the EB by asking Allison directly who signed off on the FY budget for procurement tech and requesting an intro, ideally before shortlist stage.

Avoma note uuid 2b110d2e-79b5-4e42-95f4-47888d9f2551: 'Agreement reached to execute an NDA and include Zycus in the May RFP process, aiming for Q3 implementation once the full RFP timeline is defined'
decision_process.narrative: Buyer is running a formal, multi-stage RFI → RFP → shortlist → selection process; approval chain and final sign-off mechanics not yet disclosed. The visible process to date: (1) Discovery and vendor outreach phase (Apr 2026) — Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) and Asad Rizvi (Data Analyst) led initial calls with multiple vendors including Zycus; (2) NDA execution (committed 07 Apr, legal review by Barnes & Noble, executed by mid-May inferred from RFI release); (3) RFI release 11 May 2026, due 08 Jun 2026 — Zycus submitted intent to bid 12 May per Next Step; (4) RFP planned for 'May' per 07 Apr call, likely now Jun-Jul window following RFI close; (5) Target implementation 'Q2-Q3 mid-to-late fall 2026' per Allison. Allison stated on the call 'we don't have an exact date yet' for the RFP and 'once we have a firm timeline for the RFP, we'll share that,' indicating the buyer is still finalizing the formal evaluation calendar. No mention of: who scores the RFI/RFP, whether a shortlist demo/workshop follows, whether an evaluation committee exists beyond Allison + Asad, or who holds final approval authority (see EB gap above). The call sequence itself shows only Allison (Manager level) and Asad (Analyst level) engaged so far — no escalation to VP, Director, or C-suite, and no cross-functional participants (IT, Finance, Legal) on the 07 Apr call. This is consistent with early Formal Evaluation stage but signals single-threading risk. Inference: process is buyer-controlled and formal (a positive), but lacks visibility into the approval chain and committee composition. Next move: during RFP or shortlist phase, ask Allison to walk through the evaluation committee, scoring process, and final sign-off steps, and request intros to other committee members (IT for integration vetting, Finance/EB for commercials, Legal if contract terms will be negotiated early).

sources: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Allison Baker statements on modularity, low-IT-burden, phase-able roadmap, Oracle integration, scalability, Waterstones harmonization

Avoma note uuid 8d213f10-d416-4453-a256-8ce520cb8307: 'A modular, standalone platform with minimal IT integration effort is preferred to begin with eSourcing and contract management, then scale out to wider Source-to-Pay capabilities'
decision_criteria.narrative: Core evaluation criteria explicitly stated by the buyer; formal RFI/RFP criteria not yet disclosed. On the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call, Allison Baker articulated the Must-Have and Nice-to-Have lens Barnes & Noble is applying: (1) Modular, standalone platform — 'something that can be kind of standalone that doesn't require a ton of integrations into a bunch of IT things'; (2) Low IT burden — she emphasized 'Barnes & Noble is very old school, everything's manual, spreadsheets… we're looking for something that doesn't require a ton of integrations'; (3) Phase-able roadmap — 'we really want to start with the eSourcing piece and then essentially grow out from there… baby steps'; (4) eSourcing as the day-one module, followed by CLM and P2P; (5) Integration capability with the relevant stakeholder (their core ERP) — she asked 'is that something you've done before with other customers?' and Hunsinger confirmed Zycus iSaaS approach (80% handled by Zycus, 20% by customer IT) which she responded positively to; (6) Scalability to support $500M+ unsourced spend with a 4-person team; (7) Harmonization potential with Waterstones (UK sister company, half the size) — 'in a perfect world' they would standardize platforms across both entities. She also noted 'we want to make sure along for that journey with us,' signaling partnership orientation and vendor stability/support as criteria. The RFI released 11 May will carry the formal scoring criteria; Zycus has not yet seen it as of this sweep. Inference: Zycus modular approach, iSaaS integration model, and eSourcing-first roadmap align well with stated criteria; the RFI response (due 08 Jun, submitted on time per Next Step) should mirror this fit. Next move: once RFI is scored and shortlist is announced, request the detailed evaluation scorecard and any gaps B&N flagged, then tailor the RFP response and demo to close those gaps.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS (AI Score/Status/Why) data in Salesforce for this opp (fields read null). Buyer expressed interest in AI-driven capabilities on the 07 Apr discovery call — specifically, Allison Baker responded positively to Hunsinger's introduction of agentic AI for tail-spend/auction automation: 'we're retail, I feel like there's a lot of benefit an auction platform could potentially bring us.' She also demonstrated openness to innovation and technology modernization (stated goal is to bring in eSourcing platform to replace manual/spreadsheet processes, and she has prior experience implementing procurement-tech at two companies). However, AI was NOT a primary evaluation criterion she articulated — her Must-Haves were modularity, low IT burden, Oracle integration, and scalability. AI/Merlin was positioned as a differentiator by Zycus, and Allison showed curiosity, but she did not anchor her buying decision to AI appetite. Inference based on the call: AI CURIOUS — open to AI-driven efficiency and automation (especially for tail-spend and RFx productivity), but not leading with AI as a strategic imperative or a scored RFI/RFP criterion (contrast with an 'AI Hungry' buyer who would ask 'how does your AI work? show me the ROI on AI features'). Recommendation: in RFP response and shortlist demo, tie Merlin/AI directly to her articulated pain (manual processes, throughput constraint, $500M opportunity) with quantified impact (time savings, incremental savings), so AI becomes the means to her end (scale, efficiency, savings), not a standalone feature pitch.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 07 Apr 2026 following discovery call same day. NDA process initiated and executed by mid-May (inferred from RFI release 11 May). RFI released 11 May, due 08 Jun; Zycus submitted intent to bid 12 May per Next Step dated 08 Jun. LastActivityDate 09 Jun (1 day after RFI due date), likely reflecting RFI close or internal activity log. No buyer call or email since 07 Apr discovery (79-day gap), but this is explained by buyer's internal RFI-build cycle and is NOT a stall — Allison stated on 07 Apr 'we don't have an exact date yet' for the RFP and 'once we have a firm timeline we'll share that,' and she delivered the RFI on the promised May timeline. Current phase: RFI scoring in progress (post-08-Jun), awaiting shortlist announcement and RFP release (likely late Jun to Jul 2026). Movement is buyer-paced and on track with no slip signals.

items.detail: Economic Buyer unmapped — budget approved for FY starting May 2026, but the individual with commercial sign-off authority (likely June Johnson VP Vendor Relations & AP, or a finance/operations executive) has not been identified or engaged. Without EB access, pricing and final contract approval will stall at later stages. the deal owner's manager (Manager) stated 'we did get a budget approved' but did not name who approved it. No VP, Director, or C-suite stakeholder has surfaced in any call, contact role, or Salesforce activity.

Single-threading risk — only Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) and Asad Rizvi (Data Analyst) engaged; no IT, Finance, Legal, or executive sponsor visible. For a $250k platform purchase with Oracle integration requirements and a multi-year phase-able roadmap, the evaluation committee should include IT (integration/architecture vetting), Finance (ROI validation), and executive sponsorship. Current engagement is narrowly concentrated at Manager and Analyst level, creating consensus and approval risk.

IT gatekeeper not engaged — Allison stated Oracle Financials integration is critical ('Oracle Financial is going to stay there, that's not gonna move') and asked whether Zycus has done it before; Hunsinger confirmed experience and introduced iSaaS approach, which Allison acknowledged positively. However, Scott Konzelmann (Senior Director SAP Business Systems, the IT lead for ERP/business systems per account contact list) has not been brought into the evaluation. IT will likely gate the purchase on integration feasibility, architecture fit, and resource commitment, but this vetting has not occurred yet. Risk: late-cycle IT objection or integration-complexity concern could delay or derail the deal.

Competitive landscape unknown — RFI released 11 May to multiple vendors (Allison stated 'we are reaching out to different players in the market'), but Zycus does not know who else is being evaluated, how many vendors are in the RFI, or what the buyer's evaluation scorecard looks like. RFI scoring is in progress post-08 Jun; shortlist announcement pending (likely late Jun to mid-Jul). Without visibility into the competitive field and Zycus's RFI score vs. peers, it is impossible to assess win probability or tailor the RFP response to widen the lead. Risk: a strong incumbent or a better-fit competitor could be advancing silently.

champion_strength.source: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Next Step 08 Jun 2026, LastActivityDate 09 Jun 2026
champion_strength.summary: Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) is a confirmed, active champion with strong business-buyer authority, procurement-platform buying experience, and clear advocacy for Zycus ('Zycus checks the boxes'). She personally led the 07 Apr discovery call, articulated the pain and business case unprompted, committed to include Zycus in the RFP, and drove the RFI release on her stated timeline (11 May). She has budget (FY approved) and a mandate to build the procurement function from scratch. Risks: (1) Manager-level, not VP/C-suite — likely does not hold final commercial sign-off authority (see EB gap); (2) Single-threading — only Allison and Asad Rizvi (Analyst) engaged, no IT/Finance/Legal/executive sponsor visible; (3) Long gap between discovery call (07 Apr) and next buyer touch (RFI due 08 Jun, 62 days) is explained by buyer's internal RFI-build cycle, not a cooling signal, but creates latency risk. Current engagement: ACTIVE — she is driving the RFI/RFP process on schedule. Strength: DEVELOPING — strong business champion with budget and advocacy, but unproven C-suite access and no multi-threading yet, so her ability to shepherd the deal through final EB/CFO approval and cross-functional consensus is not demonstrated. Next move: ask Allison for intros to the EB (likely June Johnson VP or the exec who approved the FY budget), IT (Scott Konzelmann), and Finance (Jennifer Banks or Arnaud Lingelser) during RFP or shortlist phase to broaden support and de-risk the single-thread dependency.

Also confirms whether Zycus advanced to shortlist, gating all subsequent moves.

items.action: Multi-thread to IT (Scott Konzelmann, Senior Director SAP Business Systems) for Oracle Financials integration and architecture vetting discussion.
items.trigger: Allison stated on 07 Apr 2026 'Oracle Financial is going to stay there, that's not gonna move' and asked about Zycus's integration experience; Hunsinger confirmed iSaaS approach, but no IT stakeholder has been engaged to vet integration feasibility, scope, or resource commitment.
items.expected_effect: Brings IT into the evaluation early (before RFP or shortlist demo), validates that Zycus's iSaaS integration approach (80% Zycus, 20% customer IT) addresses Barnes & Noble's low-IT-burden requirement and Oracle Financials architecture, and de-risks late-cycle IT objection or integration-complexity concern that could delay or derail the deal. Also builds consensus beyond Allison (Manager) and Asad (Analyst). Positioning: 'Allison, given the Oracle integration is critical, it would be helpful to bring your IT lead (Scott Konzelmann) into the conversation to walk through our iSaaS approach and confirm the integration scope and effort — can you facilitate an intro?'

items.action: Co-develop a quantified savings and ROI model tied to Barnes & Noble's $500M unsourced-spend opportunity, 4-person team constraint, and manual-process cost, and deliver it to Allison Baker as a 1-page business-case summary.
items.trigger: Allison articulated the pain and savings opportunity on 07 Apr 2026 ('north of half a billion dollars in indirect spend that has not been strategically sourced… we can bring more savings to the company for the bottom line'), but no specific savings target, payback period, or ROI commitment has been built.
items.expected_effect: Arms Allison (and the EB, once mapped) with a defensible business case to justify the $250k investment internally, tying Zycus platform ROI to measurable outcomes (e.g. '3-5% savings on $500M spend = $15-25M over 3 years, payback in 6 months'; '50% reduction in RFx cycle time frees 2 FTEs to focus on strategic sourcing'). Strengthens Zycus's value story vs. competitors and moves the conversation from feature fit to business impact. Deliver during RFP response or shortlist phase as a co-created artifact (work with Allison to validate assumptions and inputs, so she owns it).

items.action: Tie Merlin AI and agentic tail-spend automation directly to Allison's core pain (manual RFx build, throughput constraint, $500M opportunity) in the RFP response or shortlist demo, with quantified impact examples.
items.trigger: Allison showed interest in auction/tail-spend automation on 07 Apr 2026 ('we're retail, I feel like there's a lot of benefit an auction platform could bring us'), but AI/Merlin was positioned as a differentiator, not the answer to her core pain (manual processes, 4-person team, throughput).
items.expected_effect: Activates Zycus's AI wedge by making Merlin/agentic AI the solution to Allison's stated resource constraint and unsourced-spend problem, not a feature tour. Example framing: 'Your 4-person team is handling $500M+ spend manually — our GenAI RFx templates cut event-build time by 50%, and our autonomous negotiation agents can source 200+ tail-spend categories per year with zero labor, capturing 5-10% savings per category. That frees your team to focus on the strategic $500M opportunity.' Deliver this in the RFP response narrative and as a live demo use-case during shortlist, using Barnes & Noble's actual spend categories (if available) or retail-relevant examples (MRO, marketing services, facilities, IT). Differentiates Zycus vs. legacy platforms (Ariba, Oracle, Workday) that lack agentic AI.

items: Buyer drove RFI release on committed timeline — 11 May release per 07 Apr promise ('we're planning to release potentially next month'), demonstrating follow-through and active deal ownership by Allison Baker
Zycus submitted intent to bid on time (12 May), one day after RFI release, signaling responsiveness and buyer confirmed 'Zycus checks the boxes' fit on 07 Apr call
LastActivityDate 09 Jun (17 days ago) is within acceptable lag for RFI scoring window post-08 Jun due date; no ghost/dark signal
Budget approved for FY starting May 2026, and Allison stated urgency with Q2-Q3 fall 2026 implementation target
No slip in buyer's stated timeline, no negative fit feedback, no competitor named as preferred, no pricing/commercial pushback (not yet in that phase)
confidence_signals.summary: Deal is healthy and on track with no cooling signals. Positive momentum indicators: (1) Buyer drove RFI release on stated timeline (11 May, per 07 Apr commitment); (2) Zycus submitted intent to bid on time (12 May); (3) LastActivityDate 09 Jun (17 days ago) is within acceptable lag for RFI scoring window, not a stall; (4) Allison Baker confirmed strong fit on 07 Apr call ('Zycus checks the boxes') and committed to include Zycus in RFP; (5) Budget approved for FY starting May 2026, and Allison has mandate and urgency (targeting Q2-Q3 fall 2026 implementation). Long gap between discovery call (07 Apr) and RFI due date (08 Jun, 62 days) is explained by buyer's internal RFI-build process, not disengagement — Allison stated on 07 Apr 'we don't have an exact date yet' for the RFP and delivered it in the promised May window. No negative signals: no slip in buyer's timeline, no competitor named as preferred, no pushback on fit or pricing (not yet discussed), no ghost/dark period (17 days since last activity is normal for RFI-scoring phase). Risk factors are structural (EB unmapped, single-threading, competitive RFI in flight) but not sentiment-based. Cooling risk: LOW.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 07 Apr 2026, 80 days old. Stage Formal Evaluation since at least 09 Jun (17 days minimum time-in-stage measured from LastActivityDate). RFI released 11 May 2026, due 08 Jun, Zycus submitted intent to bid 12 May. Days to close: 278. Typical enterprise procurement-tech cycle for this deal size and complexity: discovery (done, 07 Apr), RFI/RFP (in flight, Jun-Jul ~4-6 weeks), shortlist + deeper demos (Aug-Sep ~6 weeks), commercials + proposal (Oct ~3 weeks), contract + legal (Nov ~3 weeks) = close realistic Dec 2026 to Jan 2027 (~6-9 months from creation). Current forecast Mar 2027 is 11 months out, roughly 2-4 months of excess buffer. No material slippage signals in the record; buyer is engaged, NDA executed, RFI submitted on time. Date is defensible as ultra-conservative placeholder but not realistic on current momentum.
evidence: LastActivityDate 09 Jun 2026 (17 days ago), within acceptable lag for RFI window
Next Step dated 08 Jun 2026: RFI released 11 May, due 08 Jun, Zycus intent to bid submitted 12 May — on schedule

Discovery call 07 Apr 2026: buyer articulated $500M unsourced indirect spend, 4-person team, manual processes, fiscal-year start May, targeting Q2-Q3 (mid-to-late fall 2026) implementation per Allison Baker
NDA process initiated 07 Apr call, buyer committed to include Zycus in May RFP
No EB, DM, or executive sponsor mapped; no commercials discussed; no pricing, references, or contract activity — all consistent with early Formal Evaluation
Stage-appropriate health: buyer is driving (released RFI on their timeline), Zycus responded, no competitor named, modular fit confirmed
north_star_verdict.headline: RFI submitted on time 08 June, but 17 days of silence in multi-vendor formal evaluation with no champion, no EB mapping, and zero visibility into shortlist timing or competitive position. Buyer drove early pace; radio silence post-submission signals stall risk.
north_star_verdict.recommended_forecast: Pipeline (appropriate — still early RFI stage, no EB mapped, no commercials yet), but close date should be pulled in to Dec 2026 or Jan 2027 to reflect realistic 8-9 month cycle.

best_practice_check.summary: Three high-impact levers to advance this healthy, early-stage deal: (1) Map the Economic Buyer and secure executive sponsorship before commercials begin — budget is approved but the sign-off authority (likely June Johnson VP or a finance exec) is unmapped, and without EB access pricing and contract negotiation cannot proceed; asking Allison for an intro to the FY-budget approver is the unlock. (2) Multi-thread beyond Allison Baker (Manager) and Asad Rizvi (Analyst) — bring IT (Scott Konzelmann, Senior Director SAP Business Systems, for Oracle integration vetting), Finance (Jennifer Banks Director FP&A or Arnaud Lingelser Finance Director, for ROI/business-case validation), and the EB into the process during shortlist/RFP phase to de-risk the single-threading dependency and build consensus across the buying committee. (3) Co-develop a quantified savings/ROI model tied to the $500M unsourced-spend opportunity and the 4-person team constraint — Allison has articulated the pain and the savings opportunity in qualitative terms, but no business case with a specific savings target, payback period, or ROI has been built; delivering this during RFP or shortlist will strengthen Zycus's value story and arm Allison (and the EB, once mapped) to justify the investment internally.

competitive_position.summary: Competitive landscape unknown — no competitor named by buyer or logged in Salesforce. Allison Baker stated on 07 Apr 2026 'we are reaching out to different players in the market' and 'we're not married to anyone,' confirming a multi-vendor RFI/RFP process is underway, but she did not disclose who else is being evaluated. RFI released 11 May to multiple vendors; shortlist will be announced post-scoring (likely late Jun to mid-Jul 2026). Probable field based on buyer's stated platform experience (Oracle, Ariba, Workday) and the eSourcing+CLM+Analytics scope: SAP Ariba, Oracle Procurement Cloud, Workday Strategic Sourcing, Coupa, Jaggaer, GEP, and potentially Zip, Ivalua, or Bonfire. Zycus's positioning: modular/phase-able approach aligns with buyer's stated preference ('start with eSourcing, grow out from there'), iSaaS integration model (80% handled by Zycus) addresses buyer's low-IT-burden requirement, and agentic AI for tail-spend automation differentiates vs. legacy platforms. Buyer confirmed on 07 Apr 'Zycus checks the boxes of what we're looking for.' Threat level for the field: MEDIUM (competitive RFI, but Zycus has strong fit and early champion advocacy). Single strongest current threat: UNKNOWN until shortlist disclosed. Wedge: modular simplicity + low IT burden + AI-driven tail-spend coverage, vs. the heavier, suite-driven legacy platforms Allison has worked with before.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI/Merlin positioning introduced and received with interest, but not yet central to the value story. On the 07 Apr 2026 discovery call, Hunsinger introduced Zycus's AI differentiation twice: (1) Agentic AI for tail-spend sourcing — 'We have an autonomous negotiation agent… for tail spend… it can automate that whole process for you to the degree you're comfortable with… it's like a gas pedal and a brake… it's capable of running all those auctions for you.' Allison responded with interest: 'we're retail, I feel like there's a lot of benefit an auction platform could potentially bring us… it's something I've never used personally… but I feel like there's a lot of benefit.' (2) GenAI for RFx question generation — 'I can literally ask an AI agent: what are the 20 best questions I should ask a vendor providing this?' Allison did not respond directly to this point. Hunsinger also positioned Zycus's 25-year AI innovation history and Merlin platform as core differentiation early in the pitch. However, Allison's primary decision lens remained modular fit, low IT burden, Oracle integration, and scalability — AI was a secondary, nice-to-have capability, not a Must-Have criterion. The RFI/RFP evaluation criteria (not yet disclosed) will reveal whether AI/innovation is a scored dimension. Under-positioned risk: MEDIUM — Allison showed curiosity about auctions/tail-spend automation, but Zycus has not yet tied AI/Merlin to her CORE pain (manual RFx build, $500M unsourced spend, 4-person team throughput constraint). The wedge is there but not yet activated. Next move: in the RFP response or shortlist demo, tie Merlin/agentic AI directly to Allison's stated pain — show how GenAI-driven RFx templates and autonomous tail-spend sourcing free up her 4-person team to focus on the strategic $500M opportunity, delivering measurable time savings (e.g. '50% reduction in RFx cycle time') and incremental savings capture (e.g. 'automate 200+ tail-spend categories per year, 5-10% savings per category'). Make AI the answer to her resource-constraint problem, not a feature tour.

items.quote: Allison Baker: 'we really want to start with the eSourcing piece and then essentially grow out from there… it sounds like iContract is able to do that and is set up to do that, which is great, and there's a lot of ability to expand.'
items.criterion: Modular, phase-able platform (start with eSourcing, grow to CLM and P2P over time)

items.quote: Allison Baker: 'we're looking for something that can be kind of standalone that doesn't require a ton of integrations into a bunch of IT things… Barnes & Noble is very old school, everything's manual.' Hunsinger: 'We have iSaaS, integration as a service — Zycus does 80% of the integration, you and your team do 20%.' Allison: 'Perfect, that's great.'
items.criterion: Low IT integration burden (minimal IT involvement, standalone capability)

items.quote: Allison Baker: 'Oracle Financial is going to stay there, that's not gonna move. Is that something you've done before with other customers?' Hunsinger: 'Yeah, literally hundreds of times… Oracle is definitely one of the partners with SAP and all the others.' Allison: 'Perfect, perfect.'
items.criterion: Oracle Financials integration experience and capability

items.quote: Allison Baker: 'north of half a billion dollars in indirect spend, small team, they're not keen to increase headcount… we need tools for faster sourcing and savings delivery.' Hunsinger introduced autonomous negotiation agents for tail-spend automation, which Allison acknowledged as relevant.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 07 Apr 2026, Avoma note uuid 7bcbf2af
items.criterion: Scalability to support $500M+ unsourced spend with a small (4-person) team, no headcount growth

items.quote: Allison Baker: 'we're retail, I feel like there's a lot of benefit an auction platform could potentially bring us… it's something I've never used personally… but since we're retail I feel like there's a lot of benefit.' Hunsinger: 'We do have an autonomous negotiation agent… for tail spend… it can automate that whole process for you.' Allison expressed interest but did not commit.

Allison Baker: 'that would be the end state in a perfect world… if we're using this platform, Waterstones should also… they're gonna follow us here.' Hunsinger noted Zycus has global offices and EMEA presence.
items.criterion: Potential for Waterstones (UK sister company) harmonization and global platform standardization

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong alignment on stated evaluation criteria. Buyer explicitly confirmed fit on 07 Apr 2026 call: 'Zycus checks the boxes of what we're looking for.' Key fit dimensions: (1) Modular approach — Zycus's building-block platform matches buyer's 'start with eSourcing, then grow' strategy; (2) Low IT burden — Zycus iSaaS integration (80% vendor, 20% customer) directly addresses buyer's concern about heavy integrations in an old-school, on-prem environment; (3) Oracle Financials integration — Hunsinger confirmed 'literally hundreds of times' Oracle integration experience, which Allison acknowledged positively; (4) Scalability — Zycus's agentic AI for tail-spend automation aligns with buyer's need to handle $500M+ spend with a 4-person team; (5) Phase-able roadmap — eSourcing → CLM → P2P sequence matches Zycus's modular go-to-market. Partial fit / to-be-validated: (6) Waterstones harmonization — Allison said 'in a perfect world' they'd standardize with UK sister company, but this is aspirational, not a hard requirement; Zycus's global footprint and EMEA presence support it. (7) Auction capability — Allison expressed interest ('we're retail, I feel like there's a lot of benefit an auction platform could bring us'), and Hunsinger introduced autonomous negotiation agents; buyer was intrigued but did not commit. No exposed gaps stated on the 07 Apr call. RFI scoring will reveal any gaps the evaluation committee identified; Zycus should request feedback once shortlist is announced.

hard.ais_why:
• Salesforce strategy spells it out: stress 'the workforce multiplier for agentic AI especially ANA' and use CLM to cut contract cycle times 70%.
• On the April 7 intro, B&N agreed an RFI would land in May, with autonomous negotiation framed as the way a four-person team can tackle $500M of unsourced indirect spend.
• RFI was issued 5/11 with intent-to-bid already submitted; active formal evaluation tied directly to ANA gives high confidence.

Without visibility into the competitive field and Zycus's RFI score vs. peers, it is impossible to assess win probability or tailor the RFP response to widen the lead. Move: ask Allison directly who signed off on the FY procurement-tech budget and request an intro before shortlist stage, positioning it as validating strategic fit and ROI at the executive level.

value.flag: Single-threading risk — only Allison Baker (Manager, Indirect Procurement) and Asad Rizvi (Data Analyst) engaged; no IT, Finance, Legal, or executive sponsor visible. For a $250k platform buy with Oracle integration and a phase-able multi-year roadmap, the evaluation committee should include IT (integration vetting), Finance (ROI validation), and executive sponsorship. Move: during RFP or shortlist, ask Allison for intros to the relevant stakeholder (Senior Director SAP Business Systems) for Oracle integration discussion, and to the relevant stakeholder (Director FP&A) or Arnaud Lingelser (Finance Director) for business-case validation, and seek an executive sponsor (June Johnson VP or equivalent).

value.flag: No quantified business case — Allison articulated $500M+ unsourced spend and 4-person team constraint as the pain, and stated 'we can bring more savings to the company for the bottom line,' but no specific savings target, cycle-time reduction goal, payback period, or ROI commitment has been built. Move: co-develop a savings/ROI model during RFP response or shortlist phase, tying Zycus platform ROI to their $500M opportunity, current manual-process cost (labor, delayed sourcing, lost savings), and the scalability/tail-spend-automation value Hunsinger introduced (agentic AI). Deliver this as a 1-page business-case summary Allison (and the EB, once mapped) can present internally to justify the investment.
